draft.introduction: John of Damascus’s Fount of Knowledge (c. 743 CE) is a systematic theological encyclopedia in three parts: philosophy, heresies, and orthodox faith. It became the standard theological textbook of the Eastern Church and influenced Aquinas in the West.

translation.original_ref: John of Damascus, Fount of Knowledge, Prologue
translation.text: I will say nothing of my own. I will gather together into one whole the things that have been worked out by the most eminent of teachers, organizing them by topic. I will set forth first what pertains to philosophy, for philosophy is the handmaiden of theology. Then I will catalog the heresies. And third, I will expound the orthodox faith.

translator_notes.note: John’s modest claim to originality—“I will say nothing of my own”—belies the work’s significance as the first systematic theology. His integration of Aristotelian categories into Christian doctrine prefigured scholasticism. Writing under Islamic rule in Damascus, he also produced the first Christian critique of Islam.
